AI.tldr: NFD is a new paradigm where AI agents are grown through conversational interaction with domain experts, using Knowledge Crystallization Cycles to transform tacit knowledge into structured assets, rather than pre-encoding all expertise upfront.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to building domain-expert AI agents (code-first and prompt-first) treat agent construction as a discrete engineering phase before deployment, which fundamentally mismatches the nature of domain expertise that is tacit, personal, and continuously evolving.
AI.method: Proposes Nurture-First Development with: 1) Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture organizing knowledge by volatility and personalization, 2) Knowledge Crystallization Cycle with formal definitions of operations and metrics, and 3) operational framework with Dual-Workspace Pattern and Spiral Development Model.
AI.result: Illustrated through a detailed case study on building a financial research agent for U.S. equity analysis, demonstrating the paradigm's application in practice.
AI.conclusion: NFD offers a more natural approach to human-agent co-evolution by allowing agents to progressively grow through interaction rather than requiring complete upfront expertise encoding, with important implications for how domain expertise can be captured and evolved in AI systems.

title: Stochastic Port-Hamiltonian Neural Networks: Universal Approximation with Passivity Guarantees
summary: Stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems represent open dynamical systems with dissipation, inputs, and stochastic forcing in an energy based form. We introduce stochastic port-Hamiltonian neural networks, SPH-NNs, which parameterize the Hamiltonian with a feedforward network and enforce skew symmetry of the interconnection matrix and positive semidefiniteness of the dissipation matrix. For Itô dynamics we establish a weak passivity inequality in expectation under an explicit generator condition, stated for a stopped process on a compact set. We also prove a universal approximation result showing that, on any compact set and finite horizon, SPH-NNs approximate the coefficients of a target stochastic port-Hamiltonian system with $C^2$ accuracy of the Hamiltonian and yield coupled solutions that remain close in mean square up to the exit time. Experiments on noisy mass spring, Duffing, and Van der Pol oscillators show improved long horizon rollouts and reduced energy error relative to a multilayer perceptron baseline.

AI.tldr: SPH-NNs are neural networks that learn stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems, enforcing physical constraints to improve long-term stability and energy accuracy in noisy dynamical systems.
AI.motivation: To develop neural networks that can learn stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems while preserving their energy-based structure and physical properties like passivity, which is important for long-term stability and accurate simulation of noisy dynamical systems.
AI.method: Propose Stochastic Port-Hamiltonian Neural Networks (SPH-NNs) that parameterize the Hamiltonian with a feedforward network while enforcing skew symmetry of the interconnection matrix and positive semidefiniteness of the dissipation matrix. The approach includes theoretical analysis of weak passivity inequality in expectation and a universal approximation theorem.
AI.result: Theoretical results include a weak passivity inequality in expectation under explicit generator conditions and a universal approximation theorem showing SPH-NNs can approximate target stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems with C² accuracy. Experiments on noisy mass spring, Duffing, and Van der Pol oscillators show improved long horizon rollouts and reduced energy error compared to multilayer perceptron baselines.
AI.conclusion: SPH-NNs provide a principled approach to learning stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems that preserves physical structure, leading to better long-term stability and energy accuracy in simulations of noisy dynamical systems compared to standard neural network approaches.

title: A Hybrid Knowledge-Grounded Framework for Safety and Traceability in Prescription Verification
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures.Framework for safe prescription auditing and hybrid knowledge-grounded reasoning
summary: Medication errors pose a significant threat to patient safety, making pharmacist verification (PV) a critical, yet heavily burdened, final safeguard. The direct application of Large Language Models (LLMs) to this zero-tolerance domain is untenable due to their inherent factual unreliability, lack of traceability, and weakness in complex reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce PharmGraph-Auditor, a novel system designed for safe and evidence-grounded prescription auditing. The core of our system is a trustworthy Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB), implemented under the Virtual Knowledge Graph (VKG) paradigm. This architecture strategically unifies a relational component for set constraint satisfaction and a graph component for topological reasoning via a rigorous mapping layer. To construct this HPKB, we propose the Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) algorithm, a framework that enables the co-evolution of both graph and relational schemas from medical texts. For auditing, we introduce the KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV), a new reasoning paradigm that transforms the LLM from an unreliable generator into a transparent reasoning engine. CoV decomposes the audit task into a sequence of verifiable queries against the HPKB, generating hybrid query plans to retrieve evidence from the most appropriate data store. Experimental results demonstrate robust knowledge extraction capabilities and show promises of using PharmGraph-Auditor to enable pharmacists to achieve safer and faster prescription verification.

AI.tldr: PharmGraph-Auditor is a system for safe prescription auditing using a hybrid knowledge base and LLM-based verification chain to address medication errors.
AI.motivation: Medication errors are a serious patient safety issue, and pharmacist verification is critical but burdensome. Direct LLM application is untenable due to factual unreliability, lack of traceability, and weakness in complex reasoning.
AI.method: Introduces PharmGraph-Auditor with a Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB) combining relational and graph components under Virtual Knowledge Graph paradigm. Uses Iterative Schema Refinement algorithm to construct HPKB from medical texts, and KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV) to transform LLMs into transparent reasoning engines that decompose audit tasks into verifiable queries against HPKB.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate robust knowledge extraction capabilities and show promise for enabling pharmacists to achieve safer and faster prescription verification.
AI.conclusion: PharmGraph-Auditor addresses LLM limitations for medication safety by combining hybrid knowledge representation with transparent reasoning, offering a promising approach to safer prescription verification.

title: Large Spikes in Stochastic Gradient Descent: A Large-Deviations View
summary: We analyse SGD training of a shallow, fully connected network in the NTK scaling and provide a quantitative theory of the catapult phase. We identify an explicit criterion separating two behaviours: When an explicit function $G$, depending only on the kernel, learning rate $η$ and data, is positive, SGD produces large NTK-flattening spikes with high probability; when $G<0$, their probability decays like $(n/η)^{-\vartheta/2}$, for an explicitly characterised $\vartheta\in (0,\infty)$. This yields a concrete parameter-dependent explanation for why such spikes may still be observed at practical widths.

AI.tldr: Analysis of SGD training in NTK scaling reveals a catapult phase with explicit criteria for spike probability in shallow networks.
AI.motivation: To provide a quantitative theory explaining the "catapult phase" phenomenon in SGD training of shallow networks under NTK scaling, particularly why large NTK-flattening spikes can still be observed at practical widths despite theoretical predictions.
AI.method: Analyze SGD training of a shallow, fully connected network using NTK scaling framework. Derive an explicit function G that depends on kernel, learning rate η, and data to separate two behavioral regimes.
AI.result: When G > 0, SGD produces large NTK-flattening spikes with high probability. When G < 0, spike probability decays like (n/η)^{-ϑ/2} for explicitly characterized ϑ ∈ (0,∞). This provides parameter-dependent explanation for spike observation at practical widths.
AI.conclusion: The analysis identifies concrete criteria governing the catapult phase in SGD training, explaining the persistence of NTK-flattening spikes at practical network widths through explicit parameter dependencies.

title: Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances large language models (LLMs) reasoning but severely suffers from calibration degeneration, where models become excessively over-confident in incorrect answers. Previous studies devote to directly incorporating calibration objective into existing optimization target. However, our theoretical analysis demonstrates that there exists a fundamental gradient conflict between the optimization for maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error. Building on this insight, we propose DCPO, a simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DCPO not only preserves accuracy on par with GRPO but also achieves the best calibration performance and substantially mitigates the over-confidence issue. Our study provides valuable insights and practical solution for more reliable LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: RLVR improves LLM reasoning but causes over-confidence; DCPO decouples reasoning and calibration objectives to resolve gradient conflict, achieving strong accuracy and much better calibration.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) enhances LLM reasoning but leads to severe calibration degeneration—models become over-confident in wrong answers. Prior work directly adds calibration objectives to the optimization target, but the authors identify a fundamental gradient conflict between maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error, motivating a decoupled approach.
AI.method: The authors perform theoretical analysis revealing a gradient conflict between accuracy and calibration objectives. They then propose DCPO, a simple yet effective framework that decouples reasoning and calibration optimization, rather than directly combining them.
AI.result: The proposed DCPO framework systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Extensive experiments show DCPO preserves accuracy comparable to GRPO while achieving the best calibration performance and substantially mitigating over-confidence.
AI.conclusion: DCPO provides a practical solution for reliable LLM deployment by addressing the calibration degeneration problem in RLVR. It offers valuable insights into the inherent conflict between accuracy and calibration, enabling better-calibrated LLMs without sacrificing reasoning performance.

title: Digging Deeper: Learning Multi-Level Concept Hierarchies
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Principled Design for Trustworthy AI
summary: Although concept-based models promise interpretability by explaining predictions with human-understandable concepts, they typically rely on exhaustive annotations and treat concepts as flat and independent. To circumvent this, recent work has introduced Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs) to explicitly model concept relationships, and Concept Splitting to discover sub-concepts using only coarse annotations. However, both HiCEMs and Concept Splitting are restricted to shallow hierarchies. We overcome this limitation with Multi-Level Concept Splitting (MLCS), which discovers multi-level concept hierarchies from only top-level supervision, and Deep-HiCEMs, an architecture that represents these discovered hierarchies and enables interventions at multiple levels of abstraction. Experiments across multiple datasets show that MLCS discovers human-interpretable concepts absent during training and that Deep-HiCEMs maintain high accuracy while supporting test-time concept interventions that can improve task performance.

AI.tldr: MLCS discovers multi-level concept hierarchies from top-level supervision, and Deep-HiCEMs represent these hierarchies for interpretable, accurate predictions with multi-level interventions.
AI.motivation: Concept-based models aim for interpretability by using human-understandable concepts, but they often require exhaustive annotations and treat concepts as flat and independent. Existing methods like Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs) and Concept Splitting model concept relationships but are limited to shallow hierarchies, which restricts their ability to capture complex, multi-level concept structures.
AI.method: The authors propose two key contributions: 1) Multi-Level Concept Splitting (MLCS), a method to discover multi-level concept hierarchies using only top-level supervision (coarse annotations). 2) Deep-HiCEMs, an architecture that represents these discovered hierarchies and supports interventions at multiple levels of abstraction.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that Multi-Level Concept Splitting (MLCS) can discover human-interpretable concepts that were not present during training. Additionally, Deep-HiCEMs maintain high accuracy while enabling test-time concept interventions at multiple abstraction levels, which can improve task performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MLCS and Deep-HiCEMs overcome the limitation of shallow hierarchies in concept-based models, enabling the discovery of interpretable multi-level concept hierarchies from coarse supervision and supporting effective interventions that can enhance both interpretability and task performance.

title: ES-dLLM: Efficient Inference for Diffusion Large Language Models by Early-Skipping
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) are emerging as a promising alternative to autoregressive models (ARMs) due to their ability to capture bidirectional context and the potential for parallel generation. Despite the advantages, dLLM inference remains computationally expensive as the full input context is processed at every iteration. In this work, we analyze the generation dynamics of dLLMs and find that intermediate representations, including key, value, and hidden states, change only subtly across successive iterations. Leveraging this insight, we propose \textbf{ES-dLLM}, a training-free inference acceleration framework for dLLM that reduces computation by skipping tokens in early layers based on the estimated importance. Token importance is computed with intermediate tensor variation and confidence scores of previous iterations. Experiments on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B demonstrate that ES-dLLM achieves throughput of up to 226.57 and 308.51 tokens per second (TPS), respectively, on an NVIDIA H200 GPU, delivering 5.6$\times$ to 16.8$\times$ speedup over the vanilla implementation and up to 1.85$\times$ over the state-of-the-art caching method, while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: ES-dLLM is a training-free inference acceleration framework for diffusion large language models that skips tokens in early layers based on importance estimation, achieving significant speedups while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs offer bidirectional context capture and parallel generation potential but have expensive inference due to processing full input context at every iteration.
AI.method: Analyzes dLLM generation dynamics, finds intermediate representations change subtly across iterations, proposes ES-dLLM which skips tokens in early layers based on importance computed via intermediate tensor variation and confidence scores.
AI.result: Achieves throughput of up to 226.57 and 308.51 TPS on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B respectively, delivering 5.6× to 16.8× speedup over vanilla implementation and up to 1.85× over state-of-the-art caching method while preserving quality.
AI.conclusion: ES-dLLM effectively accelerates dLLM inference by exploiting subtle changes in intermediate representations to selectively skip unimportant tokens, achieving substantial performance gains.

title: A Survey of Weight Space Learning: Understanding, Representation, and Generation
summary: Neural network weights are typically viewed as the end product of training, while most deep learning research focuses on data, features, and architectures. However, recent advances show that the set of all possible weight values (weight space) itself contains rich structure: pretrained models form organized distributions, exhibit symmetries, and can be embedded, compared, or even generated. Understanding such structures has tremendous impact on how neural networks are analyzed and compared, and on how knowledge is transferred across models, beyond individual training instances. This emerging research direction, which we refer to as Weight Space Learning (WSL), treats neural weights as a meaningful domain for analysis and modeling. This survey provides the first unified taxonomy of WSL. We categorize existing methods into three core dimensions: Weight Space Understanding (WSU), which studies the geometry and symmetries of weights; Weight Space Representation (WSR), which learns embeddings over model weights; and Weight Space Generation (WSG), which synthesizes new weights through hypernetworks or generative models. We further show how these developments enable practical applications, including model retrieval, continual and federated learning, neural architecture search, and data-free reconstruction. By consolidating fragmented progress under a coherent framework, this survey highlights weight space as a learnable, structured domain with growing impact across model analysis, transferring, and weight generation. We release an accompanying resource at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/Awesome-Weight-Space-Learning.

AI.tldr: This survey introduces Weight Space Learning (WSL), a research direction that treats neural network weights as a structured domain for analysis and modeling, categorizing methods into Weight Space Understanding, Representation, and Generation.
AI.motivation: Most deep learning research focuses on data, features, and architectures rather than weights, but recent advances show weight space contains rich structure with organized distributions, symmetries, and embedding capabilities that could revolutionize how neural networks are analyzed, compared, and transferred.
AI.method: The paper proposes a unified taxonomy of Weight Space Learning with three core dimensions: Weight Space Understanding (studying geometry and symmetries of weights), Weight Space Representation (learning embeddings over model weights), and Weight Space Generation (synthesizing new weights via hypernetworks or generative models).
AI.result: The survey consolidates fragmented progress into a coherent framework showing how weight space analysis enables practical applications including model retrieval, continual/federated learning, neural architecture search, and data-free reconstruction, and releases accompanying resources.
AI.conclusion: Weight space should be treated as a learnable, structured domain with growing impact across model analysis, knowledge transfer, and weight generation, representing a paradigm shift from focusing solely on individual training instances to understanding weight distributions and structures.

title: Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion: An Adaptive Denoising Schedule for Accelerated Molecular Conformation Generation
summary: Recent 3D molecular generation methods primarily use asynchronous auto-regressive or synchronous diffusion models. While auto-regressive models build molecules sequentially, they're limited by a short horizon and a discrepancy between training and inference. Conversely, synchronous diffusion models denoise all atoms at once, offering a molecule-level horizon but failing to capture the causal relationships inherent in hierarchical molecular structures. We introduce Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion (EAD) to overcome these limitations. EAD is a novel diffusion model that combines the strengths of both approaches: it uses an asynchronous denoising schedule to better capture molecular hierarchy while maintaining a molecule-level horizon. Since these relationships are often complex, we propose a dynamic scheduling mechanism to adaptively determine the denoising timestep. Experimental results show that EAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in 3D molecular generation.

AI.tldr: EAD is an equivariant asynchronous diffusion model that combines asynchronous denoising with molecule-level horizon for better 3D molecular generation.
AI.motivation: Current 3D molecular generation methods have limitations: auto-regressive models suffer from short horizon and training/inference discrepancy, while synchronous diffusion models fail to capture hierarchical molecular structures' causal relationships.
AI.method: Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion (EAD) combines asynchronous denoising schedule with molecule-level horizon, using dynamic scheduling to adaptively determine denoising timesteps to handle complex relationships.
AI.result: EAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in 3D molecular generation.
AI.conclusion: EAD successfully addresses limitations of existing approaches by better capturing molecular hierarchy while maintaining full molecule perspective.

title: Rethinking Adam for Time Series Forecasting: A Simple Heuristic to Improve Optimization under Distribution Shifts
summary: Time-series forecasting often faces challenges from non-stationarity, particularly distributional drift, where the data distribution evolves over time. This dynamic behavior can undermine the effectiveness of adaptive optimizers, such as Adam, which are typically designed for stationary objectives. In this paper, we revisit Adam in the context of non-stationary forecasting and identify that its second-order bias correction limits responsiveness to shifting loss landscapes. To address this, we propose TS_Adam, a lightweight variant that removes the second-order correction from the learning rate computation. This simple modification improves adaptability to distributional drift while preserving the optimizer core structure and requiring no additional hyperparameters. TS_Adam integrates easily into existing models and consistently improves performance across long- and short-term forecasting tasks. On the ETT datasets with the MICN model, it achieves an average reduction of 12.8% in MSE and 5.7% in MAE compared to Adam. These results underscore the practicality and versatility of TS_Adam as an effective optimization strategy for real-world forecasting scenarios involving non-stationary data. Code is available at: https://github.com/DD-459-1/TS_Adam.

AI.tldr: TS_Adam improves forecasting by removing Adam's second-order bias correction to better adapt to non-stationary data with distributional drift.
AI.motivation: Traditional adaptive optimizers like Adam struggle with non-stationary forecasting due to distributional drift, as their second-order correction limits responsiveness to shifting loss landscapes.
AI.method: TS_Adam removes the second-order correction from Adam's learning rate computation while preserving the optimizer's core structure, creating a lightweight variant with no additional hyperparameters.
AI.result: TS_Adam achieves average reductions of 12.8% in MSE and 5.7% in MAE compared to Adam on ETT datasets with MICN model, improving performance across both long- and short-term forecasting tasks.
AI.conclusion: TS_Adam is a practical and versatile optimization strategy that effectively handles non-stationary data in real-world forecasting scenarios.

title: Denoising the US Census: Succinct Block Hierarchical Regression
summary:
The US Census Bureau Disclosure Avoidance System (DAS) balances confidentiality and utility requirements for the decennial US Census (Abowd et al., 2022). The DAS was used in the 2020 Census to produce demographic datasets critically used for legislative apportionment and redistricting, federal and state funding allocation, municipal and infrastructure planning, and scientific research. At the heart of DAS is TopDown, a heuristic post-processing method that combines billions of private noisy measurements across six geographic levels in order to produce new estimates that are consistent, more accurate, and satisfy certain structural constraints on the data.
  In this work, we introduce BlueDown, a new post-processing method that produces more accurate, consistent estimates while satisfying the same privacy guarantees and structural constraints. We obtain especially large accuracy improvements for aggregates at the county and tract levels on evaluation metrics proposed by the US Census Bureau.
  From a technical perspective, we develop a new algorithm for generalized least-squares regression that leverages the hierarchical structure of the measurements and that is statistically optimal among linear unbiased estimators. This reduces the computational dependence on the number of geographic regions measured from matrix multiplication time, which would be infeasible for census-scale data, to linear time. We incorporate the additional structural constraints by combining this regression algorithm with an optimization routine that extends TDA to support correlated measurements. We further improve the efficiency of our algorithm using succinct linear-algebraic operations that exploit symmetries in the structure of the measurements and constraints. We believe our hierarchical regression and succinct operations to be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: BlueDown is a new post-processing method for census data that improves accuracy over TopDown while maintaining privacy guarantees and structural constraints.
AI.motivation: The US Census Bureau's Disclosure Avoidance System (DAS) needs to balance confidentiality and utility for critical census data used for apportionment, redistricting, funding, and research. The existing TopDown method has limitations in accuracy, especially at county and tract levels.
AI.method: Developed BlueDown, a new post-processing method featuring: 1) a hierarchical regression algorithm for generalized least-squares that leverages measurement structure for statistical optimality and linear-time computation, 2) integration with optimization routines to handle structural constraints and correlated measurements (extending TDA), and 3) efficient succinct linear-algebraic operations exploiting symmetries in measurements and constraints.
AI.result: BlueDown produces more accurate and consistent estimates than TopDown while maintaining the same privacy guarantees and structural constraints. It achieves especially large accuracy improvements for aggregates at county and tract levels on US Census Bureau evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: BlueDown represents a significant improvement over the current TopDown method in the DAS, offering better accuracy with the same privacy protections. The hierarchical regression and succinct operations developed are also valuable contributions to statistical methodology with potential independent applications.

title: Hardware Efficient Approximate Convolution with Tunable Error Tolerance for CNNs
summary: Modern CNNs' high computational demands hinder edge deployment, as traditional ``hard'' sparsity (skipping mathematical zeros) loses effectiveness in deep layers or with smooth activations like Tanh. We propose a ``soft sparsity'' paradigm using a hardware efficient Most Significant Bit (MSB) proxy to skip negligible non-zero multiplications. Integrated as a custom RISC-V instruction and evaluated on LeNet-5 (MNIST), this method reduces ReLU MACs by 88.42% and Tanh MACs by 74.87% with zero accuracy loss--outperforming zero-skipping by 5x. By clock-gating inactive multipliers, we estimate power savings of 35.2\% for ReLU and 29.96\% for Tanh. While memory access makes power reduction sub-linear to operation savings, this approach significantly optimizes resource-constrained inference.

AI.tldr: Proposes soft sparsity using MSB proxy to skip negligible non-zero multiplications in CNNs, achieving 88% MAC reduction for ReLU and 75% for Tanh with no accuracy loss, enabling efficient edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Modern CNNs have high computational demands that hinder edge deployment. Traditional 'hard' sparsity (skipping mathematical zeros) loses effectiveness in deep layers or with smooth activations like Tanh.
AI.method: Uses a hardware efficient Most Significant Bit (MSB) proxy to skip negligible non-zero multiplications, integrated as a custom RISC-V instruction. Evaluated on LeNet-5 (MNIST) with clock-gating of inactive multipliers.
AI.result: The proposed 'soft sparsity' method reduces ReLU MACs by 88.42% and Tanh MACs by 74.87% with zero accuracy loss, outperforming zero-skipping by 5x. Power savings are estimated at 35.2% for ReLU and 29.96% for Tanh.
AI.conclusion: While memory access makes power reduction sub-linear to operation savings, this soft sparsity approach significantly optimizes resource-constrained inference for edge deployment.

title: CLIPO: Contrastive Learning in Policy Optimization Generalizes RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, RLVR solely relies on final answers as outcome rewards, neglecting the correctness of intermediate reasoning steps. Training on these process-wrong but outcome-correct rollouts can lead to hallucination and answer-copying, severely undermining the model's generalization and robustness. To address this, we incorporate a Contrastive Learning mechanism into the Policy Optimization (CLIPO) to generalize the RLVR process. By optimizing a contrastive loss over successful rollouts, CLIPO steers the LLM to capture the invariant structure shared across correct reasoning paths. This provides a more robust cross-trajectory regularization than the original single-path supervision in RLVR, effectively mitigating step-level reasoning inconsistencies and suppressing hallucinatory artifacts. In experiments, CLIPO consistently improves multiple RLVR baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating uniform improvements in generalization and robustness for policy optimization of LLMs. Our code and training recipes are available at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/CLIPO.

AI.tldr: CLIPO integrates contrastive learning into RLVR to address the problem of LLMs learning from process-wrong but outcome-correct reasoning steps, improving generalization and reducing hallucinations.
AI.motivation: RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards) currently only uses final answers as rewards, ignoring the correctness of intermediate reasoning steps. This leads to models learning from rollouts where the reasoning process is wrong but the final answer happens to be correct, causing hallucination and answer-copying problems that harm generalization and robustness.
AI.method: The authors propose CLIPO (Contrastive Learning mechanism incorporated into Policy Optimization), which adds contrastive learning to the RLVR framework. CLIPO optimizes a contrastive loss over successful rollouts, helping the LLM learn the invariant structure shared across different correct reasoning paths rather than just single-path supervision.
AI.result: CLIPO consistently improves multiple RLVR baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks. It demonstrates uniform improvements in generalization and robustness for policy optimization of LLMs, effectively mitigating step-level reasoning inconsistencies and suppressing hallucinatory artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating contrastive learning into RLVR through CLIPO provides better cross-trajectory regularization than the original single-path approach, addressing the core limitation of RLVR in handling process-wrong, outcome-correct scenarios. This leads to more robust reasoning capabilities in LLMs with better generalization.

title: Lost in the Middle at Birth: An Exact Theory of Transformer Position Bias
summary:
The ``Lost in the Middle'' phenomenon -- a U-shaped performance curve where LLMs retrieve well from the beginning and end of a context but fail in the middle -- is widely attributed to learned Softmax artifacts or the distance-decay of positional encodings like RoPE. This paper makes a single, precise claim: \emph{the U-shape is already present at initialization, before any training or positional encoding takes effect.} It is an inherent geometric property of the causal decoder with residual connections.
  We model multi-layer causal attention as iterated powers of the Cesàro matrix and derive the exact closed-form influence density in the continuous limit. Causal masking forces a logarithmic divergence of gradient influence at the start of the prompt (the Primacy Tail), while residual connections create an isolated $\mathcal{O}(1)$ anchor at the final token (the Recency Delta). Between these extremes lies a factorial dead zone of order $\mathcal{O}(1/(H{-}1)!)$, where $H$ is the network depth, making middle-context retrieval and training structurally hostile. We validate empirically that untrained Qwen2 and GPT-2 architectures exhibit this U-shape at Step~0, and that it is identical with or without RoPE. Comparing initialized and pretrained networks, we show that standard training does not overcome the topological valley, confirming that the U-shape persists as an architectural baseline under standard pretraining objectives.
  We do not claim that this bias is insurmountable, nor that interventions such as RoPE modifications are useless. We establish what the baseline is and where it comes from, so that future efforts to overcome it can be precisely targeted.

AI.tldr: The U-shaped "Lost in the Middle" performance curve in LLMs exists inherently at initialization due to causal decoder architecture with residual connections, not just from trained artifacts or positional encoding issues.
AI.motivation: To identify the fundamental source of the "Lost in the Middle" phenomenon where LLMs perform better at context beginning and end but fail in the middle.
AI.method: Modeled multi-layer causal attention as iterated powers of the Cesàro matrix, derived exact closed-form influence density in continuous limit, and empirically validated with untrained Qwen2 and GPT-2 architectures.
AI.result: Found U-shape present at initialization (Step 0) with identical pattern regardless of RoPE, showing causal masking creates logarithmic divergence at prompt start (Primacy Tail) and residual connections create isolated anchor at final token (Recency Delta), with factorial dead zone in middle.
AI.conclusion: The U-shape is an inherent geometric property of causal decoder architecture with residual connections, persists through standard training, and serves as architectural baseline that future interventions must overcome.

title: A neural operator for predicting vibration frequency response curves from limited data
summary: In the design of engineered components, rigorous vibration testing is essential for performance validation and identification of resonant frequencies and amplitudes encountered during operation. Performing this evaluation numerically via machine learning has great potential to accelerate design iteration and make testing workflows more efficient. However, dynamical systems are conventionally difficult to solve via machine learning methods without using physics-based regularizing loss functions. To properly perform this forecasting task, a structure that has an inspectable physical obedience can be devised without the use of regularizing terms from first principles. The method employed in this work is a neural operator integrated with an implicit numerical scheme. This architecture enables operators to learn of the underlying state-space dynamics from limited data, allowing generalization to untested driving frequencies and initial conditions. This network can infer the system's global frequency response by training on a small set of input conditions. As a foundational proof of concept, this investigation verifies the machine learning algorithm with a linear, single-degree-of-freedom system, demonstrating implicit obedience of dynamics. This approach demonstrates 99.87% accuracy in predicting the Frequency Response Curve (FRC), forecasting the frequency and amplitude of linear resonance training on 7% of the bandwidth of the solution. By training machine learning models to internalize physics information rather than trajectory, better generalization accuracy can be realized, vastly improving the timeframe for vibration studies on engineered components.

AI.tldr: A neural operator integrated with an implicit numerical scheme learns state-space dynamics from limited data to predict frequency response curves for vibration testing, achieving 99.87% accuracy with only 7% of bandwidth training data.
AI.motivation: Accelerating vibration testing for engineered components through machine learning to speed up design iteration and make testing workflows more efficient, while overcoming the difficulty of solving dynamical systems without physics-based regularizing loss functions.
AI.method: Neural operator integrated with an implicit numerical scheme that learns underlying state-space dynamics from limited data, enabling generalization to untested driving frequencies and initial conditions without using regularizing terms from first principles.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates 99.87% accuracy in predicting the Frequency Response Curve (FRC), forecasting frequency and amplitude of linear resonance while training on only 7% of the bandwidth of the solution, verified with a linear single-degree-of-freedom system.
AI.conclusion: By training machine learning models to internalize physics information rather than trajectory, better generalization accuracy can be achieved, vastly improving the timeframe for vibration studies on engineered components.

title: Mashup Learning: Faster Finetuning by Remixing Past Checkpoints
summary: Finetuning on domain-specific data is a well-established method for enhancing LLM performance on downstream tasks. Training on each dataset produces a new set of model weights, resulting in a multitude of checkpoints saved in-house or on open-source platforms. However, these training artifacts are rarely reused for subsequent experiments despite containing improved model abilities for potentially similar tasks. In this paper, we propose Mashup Learning, a simple method to leverage the outputs of prior training runs to enhance model adaptation to new tasks. Our procedure identifies the most relevant historical checkpoints for a target dataset, aggregates them with model merging, and uses the result as an improved initialization for training. Across 8 standard LLM benchmarks, four models, and two collections of source checkpoints, Mashup Learning consistently improves average downstream accuracy by 0.5-5 percentage points over training from scratch. It also accelerates convergence, requiring 41-46% fewer training steps and up to 37% less total wall-clock time to match from-scratch accuracy, including all selection and merging overhead.

AI.tldr: Mashup Learning leverages historical checkpoints from previous LLM fine-tuning runs to improve performance and accelerate convergence on new tasks through checkpoint selection and merging.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning produces many domain-specific checkpoints that are typically not reused, despite containing valuable specialized knowledge that could benefit subsequent tasks.
AI.method: Identify relevant historical checkpoints for target dataset, aggregate them through model merging, and use the merged model as initialization for training new tasks.
AI.result: Improves average downstream accuracy by 0.5-5 percentage points over training from scratch; reduces training steps by 41-46% and wall-clock time by up to 37%.
AI.conclusion: Mashup Learning provides an effective way to reuse existing training artifacts to enhance LLM adaptation to new tasks while reducing computational costs.

title: ReMix: Reinforcement routing for mixtures of LoRAs in LLM finetuning
summary: Low-rank adapters (LoRAs) are a parameter-efficient finetuning technique that injects trainable low-rank matrices into pretrained models to adapt them to new tasks. Mixture-of-LoRAs models expand neural networks efficiently by routing each layer input to a small subset of specialized LoRAs of the layer. Existing Mixture-of-LoRAs routers assign a learned routing weight to each LoRA to enable end-to-end training of the router. Despite their empirical promise, we observe that the routing weights are typically extremely imbalanced across LoRAs in practice, where only one or two LoRAs often dominate the routing weights. This essentially limits the number of effective LoRAs and thus severely hinders the expressive power of existing Mixture-of-LoRAs models. In this work, we attribute this weakness to the nature of learnable routing weights and rethink the fundamental design of the router. To address this critical issue, we propose a new router designed that we call Reinforcement Routing for Mixture-of-LoRAs (ReMix). Our key idea is using non-learnable routing weights to ensure all active LoRAs to be equally effective, with no LoRA dominating the routing weights. However, our routers cannot be trained directly via gradient descent due to our non-learnable routing weights. Hence, we further propose an unbiased gradient estimator for the router by employing the reinforce leave-one-out (RLOO) technique, where we regard the supervision loss as the reward and the router as the policy in reinforcement learning. Our gradient estimator also enables to scale up training compute to boost the predictive performance of our ReMix. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed ReMix significantly outperform state-of-the-art parameter-efficient finetuning methods under a comparable number of activated parameters.

AI.tldr: ReMix introduces a reinforcement learning-based routing mechanism for Mixture-of-LoRAs to prevent routing weight imbalance and ensure equal LoRA effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Existing Mixture-of-LoRAs routers suffer from extremely imbalanced routing weights where only one or two LoRAs dominate, limiting the expressive power and number of effective LoRAs in the model.
AI.method: Proposes Reinforcement Routing for Mixture-of-LoRAs (ReMix) with non-learnable routing weights to ensure all active LoRAs are equally effective. Uses reinforce leave-one-out (RLOO) technique as an unbiased gradient estimator for the router, treating supervision loss as reward and router as policy in reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ReMix significantly outperforms state-of-the-art parameter-efficient finetuning methods under comparable numbers of activated parameters.
AI.conclusion: The fundamental design issue of learnable routing weights in Mixture-of-LoRAs can be addressed through reinforcement learning-based routing with non-learnable weights, leading to improved performance and better utilization of multiple LoRAs.

title: DT-BEHRT: Disease Trajectory-aware Transformer for Interpretable Patient Representation Learning
summary: The growing adoption of electronic health record (EHR) systems has provided unprecedented opportunities for predictive modeling to guide clinical decision making. Structured EHRs contain longitudinal observations of patients across hospital visits, where each visit is represented by a set of medical codes. While sequence-based, graph-based, and graph-enhanced sequence approaches have been developed to capture rich code interactions over time or within the same visits, they often overlook the inherent heterogeneous roles of medical codes arising from distinct clinical characteristics and contexts. To this end, in this study we propose the Disease Trajectory-aware Transformer for EHR (DT-BEHRT), a graph-enhanced sequential architecture that disentangles disease trajectories by explicitly modeling diagnosis-centric interactions within organ systems and capturing asynchronous progression patterns. To further enhance the representation robustness, we design a tailored pre-training methodology that combines trajectory-level code masking with ontology-informed ancestor prediction, promoting semantic alignment across multiple modeling modules. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DT-BEHRT achieves strong predictive performance and provides interpretable patient representations that align with clinicians' disease-centered reasoning. The source code is publicly accessible at https://github.com/GatorAIM/DT-BEHRT.git.

AI.tldr: DT-BEHRT is a graph-enhanced transformer model for EHR data that disentangles disease trajectories by modeling diagnosis-organ interactions and capturing asynchronous progression patterns, enhanced with tailored pre-training using code masking and ontology prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing EHR modeling approaches (sequence-based, graph-based, graph-enhanced) often overlook the inherent heterogeneous roles of medical codes arising from distinct clinical characteristics and contexts, failing to properly capture disease trajectories and progression patterns.
AI.method: Proposes DT-BEHRT, a graph-enhanced sequential architecture that: 1) disentangles disease trajectories by modeling diagnosis-centric interactions within organ systems, 2) captures asynchronous progression patterns, and 3) uses tailored pre-training combining trajectory-level code masking with ontology-informed ancestor prediction for semantic alignment across modules.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DT-BEHRT achieves strong predictive performance and provides interpretable patient representations that align with clinicians' disease-centered reasoning.
AI.conclusion: DT-BEHRT effectively models disease trajectories in EHR data through explicit organ-system modeling and asynchronous progression capture, enhanced by ontology-aware pre-training, resulting in clinically interpretable representations that outperform existing approaches.

title: Actor-Accelerated Policy Dual Averaging for Reinforcement Learning in Continuous Action Spaces
summary: Policy Dual Averaging (PDA) offers a principled Policy Mirror Descent (PMD) framework that more naturally admits value function approximation than standard PMD, enabling the use of approximate advantage (or Q-) functions while retaining strong convergence guarantees. However, applying PDA in continuous state and action spaces remains computationally challenging, since action selection involves solving an optimization sub-problem at each decision step. In this paper, we propose \textit{actor-accelerated PDA}, which uses a learned policy network to approximate the solution of the optimization sub-problems, yielding faster runtimes while maintaining convergence guarantees. We provide a theoretical analysis that quantifies how actor approximation error impacts the convergence of PDA under suitable assumptions. We then evaluate its performance on several benchmarks in robotics, control, and operations research problems. Actor-accelerated PDA achieves superior performance compared to popular on-policy baselines such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). Overall, our results bridge the gap between the theoretical advantages of PDA and its practical deployment in continuous-action problems with function approximation.

AI.tldr: Actor-accelerated Policy Dual Averaging uses a learned policy network to approximate optimization sub-problems in continuous spaces, achieving faster runtimes while maintaining convergence guarantees and outperforming PPO.
AI.motivation: Policy Dual Averaging (PDA) has strong theoretical convergence guarantees with value function approximation, but its practical application in continuous state and action spaces is computationally challenging due to optimization sub-problems that must be solved at each decision step.
AI.method: Proposes actor-accelerated PDA which uses a learned policy network to approximate the solution of the optimization sub-problems, providing theoretical analysis of how actor approximation error impacts convergence, and evaluates on robotics, control, and operations research benchmarks.
AI.result: Actor-accelerated PDA achieves superior performance compared to popular on-policy baselines like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) while maintaining faster runtimes and convergence guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges the gap between the theoretical advantages of PDA and practical deployment in continuous-action problems with function approximation.

title: Rethinking the Harmonic Loss via Non-Euclidean Distance Layers
summary: Cross-entropy loss has long been the standard choice for training deep neural networks, yet it suffers from interpretability limitations, unbounded weight growth, and inefficiencies that can contribute to costly training dynamics. The harmonic loss is a distance-based alternative grounded in Euclidean geometry that improves interpretability and mitigates phenomena such as grokking, or delayed generalization on the test set. However, the study of harmonic loss remains narrow: only Euclidean distance is explored, and no systematic evaluation of computational efficiency or sustainability was conducted. We extend harmonic loss by systematically investigating a broad spectrum of distance metrics as replacements for the Euclidean distance. We comprehensively evaluate distance-tailored harmonic losses on both vision backbones and large language models. Our analysis is framed around a three-way evaluation of model performance, interpretability, and sustainability. On vision tasks, cosine distances provide the most favorable trade-off, consistently improving accuracy while lowering carbon emissions, whereas Bray-Curtis and Mahalanobis further enhance interpretability at varying efficiency costs. On language models, cosine-based harmonic losses improve gradient and learning stability, strengthen representation structure, and reduce emissions relative to cross-entropy and Euclidean heads. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/rethinking-harmonic-loss-5BAB/.

AI.tldr: The paper extends harmonic loss by exploring various distance metrics beyond Euclidean, evaluating them on vision and language models across performance, interpretability, and sustainability metrics.
AI.motivation: Cross-entropy loss has limitations including interpretability issues, unbounded weight growth, and training inefficiencies. While harmonic loss addresses some of these problems, previous research has been limited to Euclidean distance without systematic evaluation of computational efficiency or sustainability.
AI.method: The authors systematically investigate a broad spectrum of distance metrics as replacements for Euclidean distance in harmonic loss. They evaluate these distance-tailored harmonic losses on both vision backbones and large language models, using a three-way evaluation framework of model performance, interpretability, and sustainability.
AI.result: On vision tasks, cosine distances provide the best trade-off, improving accuracy while lowering carbon emissions. Bray-Curtis and Mahalanobis distances further enhance interpretability with varying efficiency costs. On language models, cosine-based harmonic losses improve gradient/learning stability, strengthen representation structure, and reduce emissions compared to cross-entropy and Euclidean heads.
AI.conclusion: Extending harmonic loss with different distance metrics offers significant benefits across multiple dimensions. The choice of distance metric should be tailored to the specific application, with cosine distance showing particular promise for both vision and language tasks in balancing performance, interpretability, and sustainability.

title: SiMPO: Measure Matching for Online Diffusion Reinforcement Learning
summary: A commonly used family of RL algorithms for diffusion policies conducts softmax reweighting over the behavior policy, which usually induces an over-greedy policy and fails to leverage feedback from negative samples. In this work, we introduce Signed Measure Policy Optimization (SiMPO), a simple and unified framework that generalizes reweighting scheme in diffusion RL with general monotonic functions. SiMPO revisits diffusion RL via a two-stage measure matching lens. First, we construct a virtual target policy by $f$-divergence regularized policy optimization, where we can relax the non-negativity constraint to allow for a signed target measure. Second, we use this signed measure to guide diffusion or flow models through reweighted matching. This formulation offers two key advantages: a) it generalizes to arbitrary monotonically increasing weighting functions; and b) it provides a principled justification and practical guidance for negative reweighting. Furthermore, we provide geometric interpretations to illustrate how negative reweighting actively repels the policy from suboptimal actions. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that SiMPO achieves superior performance by leveraging these flexible weighting schemes, and we provide practical guidelines for selecting reweighting methods tailored to the reward landscape.

AI.tldr: SiMPO is a unified diffusion RL framework that generalizes reweighting schemes using signed measures and monotonic functions, enabling effective negative reweighting and superior performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RL algorithms for diffusion policies use softmax reweighting over the behavior policy, which tends to produce over-greedy policies and fails to effectively utilize feedback from negative samples.
AI.method: SiMPO introduces a two-stage measure matching approach: 1) Construct a virtual target policy via f-divergence regularized policy optimization, relaxing the non-negativity constraint to allow a signed target measure. 2) Use this signed measure to guide diffusion or flow models through reweighted matching.
AI.result: SiMPO achieves superior performance by leveraging flexible weighting schemes, with empirical evaluations demonstrating its effectiveness. The framework also provides practical guidelines for selecting reweighting methods based on the reward landscape.
AI.conclusion: SiMPO offers a principled justification for negative reweighting, provides geometric interpretations showing how negative reweighting repels policies from suboptimal actions, and generalizes to arbitrary monotonically increasing weighting functions.

title: Improving TabPFN's Synthetic Data Generation by Integrating Causal Structure
comment: 8 pages main text, 30 pages total (including supplementary material), 27 figures. Code: https://github.com/DavideTugnoli/tabpfn-causal-synthetic
summary: Synthetic tabular data generation addresses data scarcity and privacy constraints in a variety of domains. Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Network (TabPFN), a recent foundation model for tabular data, has been shown capable of generating high-quality synthetic tabular data. However, TabPFN is autoregressive: features are generated sequentially by conditioning on the previous ones, depending on the order in which they appear in the input data. We demonstrate that when the feature order conflicts with causal structure, the model produces spurious correlations that impair its ability to generate synthetic data and preserve causal effects. We address this limitation by integrating causal structure into TabPFN's generation process through two complementary approaches: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)-aware conditioning, which samples each variable given its causal parents, and a Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph (CPDAG)-based strategy for scenarios with partial causal knowledge. We evaluate these approaches on controlled benchmarks and six CSuite datasets, assessing structural fidelity, distributional alignment, privacy preservation, and Average Treatment Effect (ATE) preservation. Across most settings, DAG-aware conditioning improves the quality and stability of synthetic data relative to vanilla TabPFN. The CPDAG-based strategy shows moderate improvements, with effectiveness depending on the number of oriented edges. These results indicate that injecting causal structure into autoregressive generation enhances the reliability of synthetic tabular data.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses limitations in TabPFN's autoregressive tabular data generation by integrating causal structure through DAG-aware conditioning and CPDAG-based strategies to reduce spurious correlations and improve synthetic data quality.
AI.motivation: TabPFN generates synthetic tabular data autoregressively, but when feature order conflicts with causal structure, it produces spurious correlations that impair synthetic data quality and causal effect preservation.
AI.method: Two approaches: 1) DAG-aware conditioning that samples each variable given its causal parents, and 2) CPDAG-based strategy for scenarios with partial causal knowledge. Evaluated on controlled benchmarks and six CSuite datasets.
AI.result: DAG-aware conditioning improves quality and stability of synthetic data relative to vanilla TabPFN across most settings. CPDAG-based strategy shows moderate improvements, with effectiveness depending on the number of oriented edges.
AI.conclusion: Injecting causal structure into autoregressive generation enhances the reliability of synthetic tabular data by reducing spurious correlations and better preserving causal effects.

title: Discovery of a Hematopoietic Manifold in scGPT Yields a Method for Extracting Performant Algorithms from Biological Foundation Model Internals
summary: We report the discovery and extraction of a compact hematopoietic algorithm from the single-cell foundation model scGPT, to our knowledge the first biologically useful, competitive algorithm extracted from a foundation model via mechanistic interpretability. We show that scGPT internally encodes a compact hematopoietic manifold with significant developmental branch structure, validated on a strict non-overlap Tabula Sapiens external panel and confirmed via frozen-head zero-shot transfer to an independent multi-donor immune panel. To isolate this geometry, we introduce a general three-stage extraction method consisting of direct operator export from frozen attention weights, a lightweight learned adaptor, and a task-specific readout, producing a standalone algorithm without target-dataset retraining. In 88-split donor-holdout benchmarks against scVI, Palantir, DPT, CellTypist, PCA, and raw-expression baselines, the extracted algorithm achieves the strongest pseudotime-depth ordering and leads on key subtype endpoints (CD4/CD8 AUROC 0.867, mono/macro AUROC 0.951). Compared to standard probing of frozen scGPT embeddings with a 3-layer MLP, the extracted head is BH-significantly better on 6/8 classification endpoints while completing a full 12-split evaluation campaign 34.5x faster with approximately 1000x fewer trainable parameters. The exported operator compresses from three pooled attention heads to a single head without statistically significant loss, and further to a rank-64 surrogate. Mechanistic interpretability of the compact operator reveals a concentrated four-factor core explaining 66.2% of ablation impact, with factors resolving into explicit T/lymphoid, B/plasma, granulocytic, and monocyte/macrophage gene programs. A supplementary second-manifold validation (intercellular communication geometry) confirms that the extraction method generalizes beyond hematopoiesis.

AI.tldr: Researchers extracted a compact hematopoietic algorithm from scGPT foundation model via mechanistic interpretability, achieving competitive performance on hematopoietic classification tasks with minimal parameters.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that biologically useful algorithms can be extracted from foundation models through mechanistic interpretability, creating compact, performant algorithms without retraining on target datasets.
AI.method: Three-stage extraction method: direct operator export from frozen attention weights, lightweight learned adaptor, and task-specific readout. Extracted standalone algorithm validated on multiple benchmarks against baseline models.
AI.result: Extracted algorithm outperformed baselines on pseudotime-depth ordering and subtype endpoints (CD4/CD8 AUROC 0.867, mono/macro AUROC 0.951). 34.5x faster evaluation with 1000x fewer parameters than standard probing. Operator compressed successfully and revealed interpretable four-factor core explaining hematopoietic programs.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models like scGPT internally encode biologically meaningful representations that can be extracted into compact, competitive algorithms via mechanistic interpretability, enabling efficient transfer to downstream tasks without retraining.

title: Estimating condition number with Graph Neural Networks
summary: In this paper, we propose a fast method for estimating the condition number of sparse matrices using graph neural networks (GNNs). To enable efficient training and inference of GNNs, our proposed feature engineering for GNNs achieves $\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{nnz} + n)$, where $\mathrm{nnz}$ is the number of non-zero elements in the matrix and $n$ denotes the matrix dimension. We propose two prediction schemes for estimating the matrix condition number using GNNs. The extensive experiments for the two schemes are conducted for 1-norm and 2-norm condition number estimation, which show that our method achieves a significant speedup over the Hager-Higham and Lanczos methods.

AI.tldr: A fast method using graph neural networks (GNNs) to estimate sparse matrix condition numbers with O(nnz + n) complexity, achieving significant speedup over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods for estimating matrix condition numbers (like Hager-Higham and Lanczos) can be computationally expensive, especially for large sparse matrices. There's a need for faster estimation techniques that can handle sparse matrix structures efficiently.
AI.method: Propose using graph neural networks (GNNs) with efficient feature engineering achieving O(nnz + n) complexity. Develop two prediction schemes for condition number estimation, where sparse matrices are represented as graphs and GNNs learn to predict condition numbers directly from the graph structure.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the GNN-based method achieves significant speedup over Hager-Higham and Lanczos methods for both 1-norm and 2-norm condition number estimation, while maintaining reasonable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: GNNs provide an efficient alternative for sparse matrix condition number estimation, offering substantial computational advantages over traditional numerical methods while leveraging the graph structure of sparse matrices.

title: Taming Score-Based Denoisers in ADMM: A Convergent Plug-and-Play Framework
summary: While score-based generative models have emerged as powerful priors for solving inverse problems, directly integrating them into optimization algorithms such as ADMM remains nontrivial. Two central challenges arise: i) the mismatch between the noisy data manifolds used to train the score functions and the geometry of ADMM iterates, especially due to the influence of dual variables, and ii) the lack of convergence understanding when ADMM is equipped with score-based denoisers. To address the manifold mismatch issue, we propose ADMM plug-and-play (ADMM-PnP) with the AC-DC denoiser, a new framework that embeds a three-stage denoiser into ADMM: (1) auto-correction (AC) via additive Gaussian noise, (2) directional correction (DC) using conditional Langevin dynamics, and (3) score-based denoising. In terms of convergence, we establish two results: first, under proper denoiser parameters, each ADMM iteration is a weakly nonexpansive operator, ensuring high-probability fixed-point $\textit{ball convergence}$ using a constant step size; second, under more relaxed conditions, the AC-DC denoiser is a bounded denoiser, which leads to convergence under an adaptive step size schedule. Experiments on a range of inverse problems demonstrate that our method consistently improves solution quality over a variety of baselines.

AI.tldr: ADMM-PnP with AC-DC denoiser addresses manifold mismatch and convergence issues when integrating score-based generative models into ADMM for inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Score-based generative models are powerful priors for inverse problems, but directly integrating them into optimization algorithms like ADMM is challenging due to: 1) mismatch between noisy data manifolds used for training and ADMM iterates geometry, and 2) lack of convergence understanding when ADMM uses score-based denoisers.
AI.method: Proposes ADMM plug-and-play (ADMM-PnP) with AC-DC denoiser: a three-stage denoiser embedded into ADMM consisting of: 1) auto-correction (AC) via additive Gaussian noise, 2) directional correction (DC) using conditional Langevin dynamics, and 3) score-based denoising.
AI.result: Establishes two convergence results: 1) under proper denoiser parameters, each ADMM iteration is a weakly nonexpansive operator ensuring high-probability fixed-point ball convergence with constant step size; 2) under more relaxed conditions, AC-DC denoiser is a bounded denoiser leading to convergence with adaptive step size schedule. Experiments show consistent improvement over baselines on various inverse problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ADMM-PnP with AC-DC denoiser effectively addresses manifold mismatch and convergence issues, providing a principled framework for integrating score-based generative models into ADMM optimization with provable convergence guarantees and improved solution quality.

title: Robust Post-Training for Generative Recommenders: Why Exponential Reward-Weighted SFT Outperforms RLHF
summary: Aligning generative recommender systems to user preferences via post-training is critical for closing the gap between next-item prediction and actual recommendation quality. Existing post-training methods are ill-suited for production-scale systems: RLHF methods reward hack due to noisy user feedback and unreliable reward models, offline RL alternatives require propensity scores that are unavailable, and online interaction is infeasible. We identify exponential reward-weighted SFT with weights $w = \exp(r/λ)$ as uniquely suited to this setting, and provide the theoretical and empirical foundations that explain why. By optimizing directly on observed rewards without querying a learned reward model, the method is immune to reward hacking, requires no propensity scores, and is fully offline. We prove the first policy improvement guarantees for this setting under noisy rewards, showing that the gap scales only logarithmically with catalog size and remains informative even for large item catalogs. Crucially, we show that temperature $λ$ explicitly and quantifiably controls the robustness-improvement tradeoff, providing practitioners with a single interpretable regularization hyperparameter with theoretical grounding. Experiments on three open-source and one proprietary dataset against four baselines confirm that exponential reward weighting is simple, scalable, and consistently outperforms RLHF-based alternatives.

AI.tldr: Exponential reward-weighted supervised fine-tuning (with weights w = exp(r/λ)) is uniquely suited for production-scale generative recommender systems, outperforming RLHF alternatives while avoiding reward hacking, requiring no propensity scores, and operating fully offline.
AI.motivation: Aligning generative recommender systems to user preferences via post-training is critical but existing methods (RLHF, offline RL alternatives, online interaction) are ill-suited for production-scale systems due to noisy user feedback, unreliable reward models, lack of propensity scores, and feasibility constraints.
AI.method: Exponential reward-weighted supervised fine-tuning with weights w = exp(r/λ), optimizing directly on observed rewards without querying a learned reward model. This method is immune to reward hacking, requires no propensity scores, and operates fully offline.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves policy improvement guarantees under noisy rewards, with gaps scaling only logarithmically with catalog size. Temperature λ explicitly controls robustness-improvement tradeoff. Experiments on three open-source and one proprietary dataset show exponential reward weighting consistently outperforms RLHF-based alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Exponential reward-weighted SFT is simple, scalable, and better suited for production-scale generative recommender systems than RLHF-based alternatives, providing practitioners with a single interpretable regularization hyperparameter with theoretical grounding.

title: Federated Active Learning Under Extreme Non-IID and Global Class Imbalance
summary: Federated active learning (FAL) seeks to reduce annotation cost under privacy constraints, yet its effectiveness degrades in realistic settings with severe global class imbalance and highly heterogeneous clients. We conduct a systematic study of query-model selection in FAL and uncover a central insight: the model that achieves more class-balanced sampling, especially for minority classes, consistently leads to better final performance. Moreover, global-model querying is beneficial only when the global distribution is highly imbalanced and client data are relatively homogeneous; otherwise, the local model is preferable. Based on these findings, we propose FairFAL, an adaptive class-fair FAL framework. FairFAL (1) infers global imbalance and local-global divergence via lightweight prediction discrepancy, enabling adaptive selection between global and local query models; (2) performs prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features to promote class-aware querying; and (3) applies a two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling strategy with k-center refinement. Experiments on five benchmarks show that FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches under challenging long-tailed and non-IID settings. The code is available at https://github.com/chenchenzong/FairFAL.

AI.tldr: FairFAL is an adaptive class-fair federated active learning framework that addresses challenges of severe global class imbalance and client heterogeneity by dynamically selecting query models, promoting class-aware sampling, and balancing uncertainty with diversity.
AI.motivation: Federated active learning (FAL) suffers from degraded effectiveness in realistic settings with severe global class imbalance and highly heterogeneous clients. Existing methods struggle with these challenging long-tailed and non-IID settings.
AI.method: FairFAL includes three key components: (1) lightweight prediction discrepancy to infer global imbalance and local-global divergence for adaptive selection between global and local query models, (2) prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features for class-aware querying, and (3) two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling with k-center refinement.
AI.result: Experiments on five benchmarks show that FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches under challenging long-tailed and non-IID settings, demonstrating superior performance in realistic federated learning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that class-balanced sampling, especially for minority classes, is crucial for effective federated active learning. FairFAL successfully addresses the limitations of current FAL methods by adapting to imbalance conditions and promoting fair querying across classes.

title: Causal Concept Graphs in LLM Latent Space for Stepwise Reasoning
summary: Sparse autoencoders can localize where concepts live in language models, but not how they interact during multi-step reasoning. We propose Causal Concept Graphs (CCG): a directed acyclic graph over sparse, interpretable latent features, where edges capture learned causal dependencies between concepts. We combine task-conditioned sparse autoencoders for concept discovery with DAGMA-style differentiable structure learning for graph recovery and introduce the Causal Fidelity Score (CFS) to evaluate whether graph-guided interventions induce larger downstream effects than random ones. On ARC-Challenge, StrategyQA, and LogiQA with GPT-2 Medium, across five seeds ($n{=}15$ paired runs), CCG achieves $\CFS=5.654\pm0.625$, outperforming ROME-style tracing ($3.382\pm0.233$), SAE-only ranking ($2.479\pm0.196$), and a random baseline ($1.032\pm0.034$), with $p<0.0001$ after Bonferroni correction. Learned graphs are sparse (5-6\% edge density), domain-specific, and stable across seeds.

AI.tldr: Causal Concept Graphs (CCG) is a method that learns interpretable causal graphs over sparse latent features to explain multi-step reasoning in language models, outperforming existing methods on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Sparse autoencoders can identify where concepts exist in language models but cannot explain how these concepts interact during multi-step reasoning processes.
AI.method: Combines task-conditioned sparse autoencoders for concept discovery with DAGMA-style differentiable structure learning for graph recovery, and introduces Causal Fidelity Score (CFS) for evaluation.
AI.result: CCG achieves CFS=5.654±0.625 on ARC-Challenge, StrategyQA, and LogiQA with GPT-2 Medium, significantly outperforming ROME-style tracing (3.382±0.233), SAE-only ranking (2.479±0.196), and random baseline (1.032±0.034) with p<0.0001.
AI.conclusion: CCG provides interpretable causal graphs that are sparse (5-6% edge density), domain-specific, and stable across seeds, offering a framework to understand concept interactions in language model reasoning.

title: GSVD for Geometry-Grounded Dataset Comparison: An Alignment Angle Is All You Need
summary: Geometry-grounded learning asks models to respect structure in the problem domain rather than treating observations as arbitrary vectors. Motivated by this view, we revisit a classical but underused primitive for comparing datasets: linear relations between two data matrices, expressed via the co-span constraint $Ax = By = z$ in a shared ambient space. To operationalize this comparison, we use the generalized singular value decomposition (GSVD) as a joint coordinate system for two subspaces. In particular, we exploit the GSVD form $A = HCU$, $B = HSV$ with $C^{\top}C + S^{\top}S = I$, which separates shared versus dataset-specific directions through the diagonal structure of $(C, S)$. From these factors we derive an interpretable *angle score* $θ(z) \in [0, π/2]$ for a sample $z$, quantifying whether z is explained relatively more by $A$, more by $B$, or comparably by both. The primary role of $θ(z)$ is as a *per-sample geometric diagnostic*. We illustrate the behavior of the score on MNIST through angle distributions and representative GSVD directions. A binary classifier derived from $θ(z)$ is presented as an illustrative application of the score as an interpretable diagnostic tool.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an interpretable angle score derived from GSVD to quantify whether a sample is explained more by dataset A, dataset B, or comparably by both, serving as a geometric diagnostic tool.
AI.motivation: To develop geometry-grounded learning methods that respect structure in problem domains by revisiting linear relations between datasets via co-span constraints, moving beyond treating observations as arbitrary vectors.
AI.method: Uses generalized singular value decomposition (GSVD) as a joint coordinate system for two subspaces, exploiting the form A = HCU, B = HSV with CᵀC + SᵀS = I to separate shared vs dataset-specific directions, then derives an interpretable angle score θ(z) ∈ [0, π/2] for each sample.
AI.result: Develops an interpretable angle score that quantifies how samples are explained by different datasets, demonstrates its behavior on MNIST through angle distributions and representative GSVD directions, and presents a binary classifier derived from θ(z) as an application.
AI.conclusion: The angle score serves as a powerful per-sample geometric diagnostic tool that provides interpretable insights into dataset relationships, enabling applications like classification while maintaining geometric interpretability.

title: Optimal Expert-Attention Allocation in Mixture-of-Experts: A Scalable Law for Dynamic Model Design
summary: This paper presents a novel extension of neural scaling laws to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, focusing on the optimal allocation of compute between expert and attention sub-layers. As MoE architectures have emerged as an efficient method for scaling model capacity without proportionally increasing computation, determining the optimal expert-attention compute ratio becomes critical. We define the ratio $r$ as the fraction of total FLOPs per token dedicated to the expert layers versus the attention layers, and explore how this ratio interacts with the overall compute budget and model sparsity. Through extensive experiments with GPT-style MoE Transformers, we empirically find that the optimal ratio $r^*$ follows a power-law relationship with total compute and varies with sparsity. Our analysis leads to an explicit formula for $r^*$, enabling precise control over the expert-attention compute allocation. We generalize the Chinchilla scaling law by incorporating this architectural parameter, providing a new framework for tuning MoE models beyond size and data. Our findings offer practical guidelines for designing efficient MoE models, optimizing performance while respecting fixed compute budgets.

AI.tldr: A novel extension of neural scaling laws to Mixture-of-Experts models that determines optimal compute allocation between expert and attention layers, with empirical power-law relationships for optimal ratios.
AI.motivation: As MoE architectures efficiently scale model capacity without proportional compute increase, determining optimal expert-attention compute ratio becomes critical for efficient model design.
AI.method: Extensive experiments with GPT-style MoE Transformers exploring how expert-attention compute ratio interacts with overall compute budget and model sparsity, defining ratio r as fraction of total FLOPs per token for expert vs attention layers.
AI.result: Empirical finding that optimal ratio r* follows power-law relationship with total compute and varies with sparsity, leading to explicit formula for r* that enables precise control over expert-attention compute allocation.
AI.conclusion: Generalizes Chinchilla scaling law by incorporating architectural parameter, provides practical guidelines for designing efficient MoE models and optimizing performance while respecting fixed compute budgets.

title: Copula-ResLogit: A Deep-Copula Framework for Unobserved Confounding Effects
